name: Current Affairs in Indian Economy & TN Dev. Admin. (11): RBI's June 2026 Monetary Policy Review

sourceRefs.label: wionews.com/marketsmithindia.com - RBI's June 2026 MPC meeting: repo rate held at 5.25%, GDP growth forecast cut to 6.6%, inflation projection raised to 5.1%

researchNotes: Fifteenth Economy current-affairs topic (11th lap). Anchored on a single dated MPC MEETING's specific outputs (not a constantly-revising monthly statistic) — framed explicitly as 'this meeting's forecast/decision' rather than asserting the forecast numbers as settled facts, distinguishing this FY27 forward-looking forecast from the FY26 actual growth figure (7.7%) already covered at Day 82. Expansion pass (Batch 23, closing out the content-expansion pass): added the neutral-vs-accommodative-vs-restrictive monetary policy stance distinction.

reading.intro: Covers the RBI's June 2026 Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting outcomes — its repo-rate decision and revised forecasts.

qa.question: What repo-rate decision did the RBI's MPC make at its June 2026 meeting?
qa.answer: Kept the repo rate UNCHANGED at 5.25% — a unanimous decision, maintaining the neutral policy stance adopted earlier in the year.

qa.question: How did the RBI revise its GDP growth forecast at this meeting?
qa.answer: Cut from 6.9% to 6.6% for the current fiscal year (a forward-looking FORECAST, distinct from the FY26 actual growth figure of ~7.7% covered separately).

qa.question: How did the RBI revise its inflation projection at this meeting?
qa.answer: Raised from 4.6% to 5.1%; core inflation (excluding food and fuel) for FY27 was also revised up, from 4.4% to 4.7%.

qa.question: What was India's retail inflation rate in May 2026, and what primarily drove it?
qa.answer: 3.9% (up from 3.5% in April 2026, the highest since January of the previous year) — driven largely by an uptick in FOOD inflation, while core inflation remained relatively benign.

qa.question: What factors does the RBI expect to push inflation higher going forward?
qa.answer: Partial pass-through of higher energy prices to domestic pump prices, rising input prices, and expectations of a lower-than-normal monsoon.

qa.question: What is a 'neutral' monetary policy stance, and how does it differ from an 'accommodative' or 'restrictive' stance?
qa.answer: NEUTRAL means the RBI is not pre-committed to either cutting or raising rates — it will move based on incoming data. ACCOMMODATIVE means a bias toward further rate CUTS to support growth. RESTRICTIVE (or 'tightening') means a bias toward further rate HIKES to control inflation. The RBI's June 2026 neutral stance signals data-dependent flexibility rather than a fixed future direction.

questions.prompt: At its June 2026 meeting, the RBI's MPC kept the repo rate at:
At this meeting, the RBI revised its GDP growth forecast for the current fiscal year from 6.9% to:
At this meeting, the RBI revised its inflation projection from 4.6% to:
India's retail inflation in May 2026 was 3.9%, primarily driven by an uptick in:
The RBI's June 2026 MPC decision on the repo rate was:
A 'neutral' monetary policy stance means the RBI is:
options: Committed to only cutting rates going forward
Committed to only raising rates going forward
Not pre-committed to either direction, moving based on incoming data
questions.explanation: Not pre-committed to either direction — data-dependent.
questions.trick: Neutral = flexible/data-dependent; accommodative = bias to cut; restrictive/tightening = bias to hike.
